section.title: Israel Settles in Egypt
section.sectionRationale: The whole household of Jacob goes down to Egypt, but not before God meets Jacob at Beersheba with a promise to go with him. A careful roll of names records the seed that will become a nation, father and son are reunited, and Israel is settled and multiplied in Goshen even as Egypt itself passes into Pharaoh's hand.
section.thesis: God confirms His covenant as He brings Israel down to Egypt, providing through Joseph so that the family becomes fruitful in Goshen while Jacob looks beyond his death to the land of promise.
section.frameNote: Six scenes: God's word at Beersheba; the roll of those who went to Egypt; the reunion of father and son; the audience with Pharaoh and settlement in Goshen; the famine years and the land of Egypt; and Israel's growth with Jacob's charge to be buried with his fathers.

units.label: God speaks at Beersheba
units.note: do not be afraid to go down to Egypt

verses.text: So Israel set out with all that he had, and when he came to Beersheba, he offered {{zevach|sacrifices}} to the God of his father Isaac.
And that night God spoke to Israel in a {{marah|vision}}: “Jacob, Jacob!” He said. “Here I am,” replied Jacob.
“I am God,” He said, “the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for I will make you into a great {{goy|nation}} there.
I will go down with you to Egypt, and I will surely bring you back. And Joseph’s own hands will close your eyes.”
Then Jacob departed from Beersheba, and the sons of Israel took their father Jacob in the wagons Pharaoh had sent to carry him, along with their children and wives.
They also took the livestock and possessions they had acquired in the land of Canaan, and Jacob and all his offspring went to Egypt.
Jacob took with him to Egypt his sons and grandsons, and his daughters and granddaughters, all his offspring.
Now these are the names of the sons of Israel (Jacob and his descendants) who went to Egypt: Reuben, Jacob’s firstborn.
The sons of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.
The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman.
The sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
The sons of Judah: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah; but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan. The sons of Perez: Hezron and Hamul.
The sons of Issachar: Tola, Puvah, Job, and Shimron.
The sons of Zebulun: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.
These are the sons of Leah born to Jacob in Paddan-aram, in addition to his daughter Dinah. The total number of sons and daughters was thirty-three.
The sons of Gad: Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.
The children of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah, and their sister Serah. The sons of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.
These are the sons of Jacob born to Zilpah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Leah, sixteen in all.
The sons of Jacob’s wife Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.
Manasseh and Ephraim were born to Joseph in the land of Egypt by Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On.
The sons of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.
These are the sons of Rachel born to Jacob, fourteen in all.
The son of Dan: Hushim.
The sons of Naphtali: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.
These are the sons of Jacob born to Bilhah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Rachel, seven in all.
All those belonging to Jacob who came to Egypt, his direct descendants, besides the wives of Jacob’s sons, numbered sixty-six persons.
And with the two sons who had been born to Joseph in Egypt, the members of Jacob’s family who went to Egypt were seventy in all.

units.label: Father and son reunited
units.note: finally I can die, now that I have seen your face

verses.text: Now Jacob had sent Judah ahead of him to Joseph to get directions to Goshen. When Jacob’s family arrived in the land of Goshen,
Joseph prepared his chariot and went there to meet his father Israel. Joseph presented himself to him, embraced him, and wept profusely.
Then Israel said to Joseph, “Finally I can die, now that I have seen your face and know that you are still alive!”
Joseph said to his brothers and to his father’s household, “I will go up and inform Pharaoh: ‘My brothers and my father’s household from the land of Canaan have come to me.
The men are shepherds; they raise livestock, and they have brought their flocks and herds and all that they own.’
When Pharaoh summons you and asks, ‘What is your occupation?’

you are to say, ‘Your servants have raised livestock ever since our youth, both we and our fathers.’ Then you will be allowed to settle in the land of Goshen, since all shepherds are detestable to the Egyptians.”
So Joseph went and told Pharaoh: “My father and my brothers, with their flocks and herds and all they own, have come from the land of Canaan and are now in Goshen.”
And he chose five of his brothers and presented them before Pharaoh.
“What is your occupation?” Pharaoh asked Joseph’s brothers. “Your servants are shepherds,” they replied, “both we and our fathers.”

Then they said to Pharaoh, “We have come to live in the land for a time, because there is no pasture for the flocks of your servants, since the famine in the land of Canaan has been severe. So now, please allow your servants to settle in the land of Goshen.”
Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Now that your father and brothers have come to you,

the land of Egypt is before you; settle your father and brothers in the best part of the land. They may dwell in the land of Goshen. And if you know of any talented men among them, put them in charge of my own livestock.”
Then Joseph brought in his father Jacob and presented him before Pharaoh, and Jacob {{barak|blessed}} Pharaoh.
“How many years have you lived?” Pharaoh asked.
“My travels have lasted 130 years,” Jacob replied. “My years have been few and hard, and they have not matched the years of the travels of my fathers.”
Then Jacob blessed Pharaoh and departed from his presence.
So Joseph settled his father and brothers in the land of Egypt and gave them property in the best part of the land, the district of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded.
Joseph also provided his father and brothers and all his father’s household with food for their families.

units.label: The famine and the land of Egypt

verses.text: There was no food, however, in all that region, because the famine was so severe; the lands of Egypt and Canaan had been exhausted by the famine.
Joseph collected all the money to be found in the land of Egypt and the land of Canaan in exchange for the grain they were buying, and he brought it into Pharaoh’s palace.
When the money from the lands of Egypt and Canaan was gone, all the Egyptians came to Joseph and said, “Give us food. Why should we die before your eyes? For our funds have run out!”
“Then bring me your livestock,” said Joseph. “Since the money is gone, I will sell you food in exchange for your livestock.”

So they brought their livestock to Joseph, and he gave them food in exchange for their horses, their flocks and herds, and their donkeys. Throughout that year he provided them with food in exchange for all their livestock.

When that year was over, they came to him the second year and said, “We cannot hide from our lord that our money is gone and all our livestock belongs to you. There is nothing left for our lord except our bodies and our land.

Why should we perish before your eyes, we and our land as well? Purchase us and our land in exchange for food. Then we, along with our land, will be slaves to Pharaoh. Give us seed that we may live and not die, and that the land may not become desolate.”
So Joseph acquired for Pharaoh all the land in Egypt; the Egyptians, one and all, sold their fields because the famine was so severe upon them. The land became Pharaoh’s,
and Joseph reduced the people to servitude from one end of Egypt to the other.
However, he did not acquire the priests’ portion of the land, for it had been given to them by Pharaoh. They ate the rations that Pharaoh supplied; so they did not sell their land.
Then Joseph said to the people, “Now that I have acquired you and your land for Pharaoh this day, here is seed for you to sow in the land.
At harvest time, you are to give a fifth of it to Pharaoh, and four-fifths will be yours as seed for the field and food for yourselves and your households and children.”
“You have saved our lives,” they said. “We have found favor in our lord’s eyes, and we will be Pharaoh’s servants.”
So Joseph established a law that a fifth of the produce belongs to Pharaoh, and it is in effect in the land of Egypt to this day. Only the priests’ land does not belong to Pharaoh.

units.label: Israel grows; Jacob's charge
units.note: do not bury me in Egypt

verses.text: Now the Israelites settled in the land of Egypt, in the region of Goshen. They acquired property there and became fruitful and increased greatly in number.
And Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years, and the length of his life was 147 years.

When the time drew near for Israel to die, he called his son Joseph and said to him, “If I have found favor in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and promise to show me kindness and faithfulness. Do not bury me in Egypt,
but when I lie down with my fathers, carry me out of Egypt and bury me with them.” Joseph answered, “I will do as you have requested.”
“Swear to me,” Jacob said. So Joseph swore to him, and Israel bowed in worship at the head of his bed.

zevach.gloss: A sacrifice, especially an animal offered to God. At the border of the promised land Jacob pauses to worship before going down to Egypt. The sacrifice frames the whole journey as an act of faith seeking God's direction.

marah.gloss: A vision, a revelatory appearance in which God makes Himself known. God meets Jacob by night in such a vision, calling his name and renewing the covenant. It marks a turning point, God's own approval of the descent into Egypt.

goy.gloss: A nation or people. God promises to make Jacob into a great nation in Egypt, echoing the pledge to Abraham. The word looks ahead to Israel's growth from a household of seventy into a mighty people.

barak.gloss: To bless, to speak good and favor over another. The aged patriarch, a mere resident alien, blesses the mighty Pharaoh, and the lesser is blessed by the greater. It quietly signals whose God is truly sovereign in the encounter.

basic.context: Jacob moves his entire family to Egypt, but first God meets him at Beersheba and promises to go with him. A roll of seventy names records the family that will become a nation, Joseph and Jacob are reunited, and Israel settles in Goshen. The key question is whether leaving the promised land means leaving the promise behind.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative with an embedded genealogy and a royal audience. The main question is how to read the descent into Egypt: is it a detour or part of God's plan? God's word in the vision settles it, this is His doing, and He will bring them back.
basic.originalLanguages: Jacob offers zevach, sacrifices, and God appears in a marah, a vision, promising to make him a great goy, nation. Later the aged Jacob speaks barak, blessing, over Pharaoh, the lesser blessing the greater, hinting who truly holds authority.
basic.typology: Israel goes down into Egypt and will one day be brought up again, a pattern the exodus fulfills and the Gospels echo when the child Jesus is called out of Egypt. Joseph's provision of bread and land foreshadows Christ's provision for His people.
basic.christ: Joseph sustains a starving world and gathers his family to safety, a picture of Christ who feeds the hungry and gathers His people. Jacob's dying insistence on burial in the promised land looks in hope beyond death to God's promise, fulfilled in the resurrection Christ secures.
basic.god: God promises, I will go down with you and I will surely bring you back. He is present with His people even in a foreign land and faithful to His covenant across generations. Behind Joseph's administration stands God's providence keeping Israel alive.
basic.crossReferences: The seventy who went down grow into the nation of Exodus 1. The promise to bring them back is fulfilled in the exodus, and Hebrews 11 commends Jacob's faith as he blesses and worships at his death.
basic.oneStory: The family of promise becomes a nation in Egypt, exactly as God said. The descent is not the end of the covenant but a chapter in it, setting up the exodus and, far ahead, the coming of the Saviour from this people.

context.literary: This movement joins narrative, a formal genealogy of seventy names, and a royal audience. The vision at Beersheba deliberately echoes God's earlier appearances to Abraham and Isaac, and the descent into Egypt is framed as covenant history, not mere migration. The land transactions in the famine round out Joseph's administrative wisdom.
context.historical: Goshen in the northeastern delta suited herdsmen and kept Israel somewhat apart from Egyptians, who disdained shepherds. Selling land and persons to the crown during prolonged famine reflects known ancient patterns of centralized grain economies, with priestly estates often exempt.
context.canonical: The number seventy and the promise of a great nation link this passage to Exodus, where Israel has multiplied. Jacob's charge to be buried with his fathers ties the family to the land of promise even while living abroad, keeping the covenant hope alive.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the descent into Egypt is God's own plan for making Israel a great nation, to record the covenant seed by name, and to display Joseph's God-given provision. The narrator wants Israel, later reading this, to see their origins and God's faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapters describe Joseph's famine policy and the settlement in Goshen; they do not prescribe an economic program for all time. The abiding truths are God's presence with His people in exile, His covenant faithfulness, and hope that reaches beyond death to the land of promise.

debates.question: How should we assess Joseph's policy of acquiring the people's land and persons for Pharaoh?

views.label: Wise and life-saving stewardship
views.case: Joseph keeps a whole population alive through years of famine, and the people themselves say, you have saved our lives; the arrangement is a fifth tax, not harsh servitude.

views.label: A troubling concentration of power
views.case: Others note the sober irony that Israel's savior centralizes Egypt under Pharaoh, foreshadowing the very power that will later enslave Israel; the text reports it without full endorsement.

debates.bearing: The narrative presents Joseph as a faithful provider in a fallen system; readers can honor his life-saving wisdom while noting how concentrated power can later turn against God's people.

study.originalLanguages: At Beersheba Jacob offers zevach, sacrifices, worshiping before he journeys, and God appears in a marah, a night vision, renewing the promise to make him a great goy, nation, in Egypt. The scene at Pharaoh's court is quietly weighted when Jacob speaks barak, blessing, over the king twice; in Scripture the greater blesses the lesser, so the frail patriarch, a landless alien, stands as the channel of God's blessing before the most powerful man on earth.

typology.type: Israel's descent into Egypt to be brought up again
typology.fulfillment: The exodus, and Christ Himself called out of Egypt, the true Israel who fulfills the pattern.

typology.type: Joseph feeding the world with bread
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the bread of life, sustaining and gathering His people.

typology.type: Jacob's hope of burial in the promised land
typology.fulfillment: Resurrection hope in Christ, who secures the inheritance beyond death.

study.christ: Joseph gathers his family into safety and feeds a starving world, a shadow of Christ who provides the true bread and gathers His scattered people to Himself. The descent into Egypt, from which God promises to bring Israel up again, sets a pattern the exodus completes and the Gospels echo when God calls His Son out of Egypt. Jacob, dying in a foreign land, refuses to let his bones rest there and clings to the land of promise, a faith that looks past death to God's future, ultimately secured in the resurrection of Jesus.
study.god: God's central word here is personal and reassuring: do not be afraid, I will go down with you to Egypt, and I will surely bring you back. He is not left behind at the border of Canaan; He accompanies His people into exile and remains faithful to His covenant across the generations. Behind Joseph's wise provision and the growth of Israel in Goshen stands the same God who keeps His promise to make a great nation and, in time, to bring them home.

crossReferences.note: God had foretold to Abraham the sojourn in a foreign land and the return, now unfolding.
The seventy who went down become a multiplying nation, fulfilling the promise.
Out of Egypt I called My son, the pattern of descent and return applied to Israel and Christ.
By faith Jacob and Joseph, near death, look to God's promise and the land.

study.oneStory: The household of promise goes down to Egypt and there begins to swell into a nation, exactly as God pledged. Far from abandoning the covenant, the descent is a planned chapter within it, guarded by God's presence and Joseph's provision. Jacob's dying hope of the promised land keeps the covenant alive and points ahead to the exodus and, beyond it, to the Saviour who will come from this people and lead a greater deliverance.

quiz.q: What does God tell Jacob in the vision at Beersheba?

options.text: Do not be afraid to go to Egypt, for I will go with you and surely bring you back.
options.note: God promises His presence in exile and a future return.

options.text: Stay in Canaan and do not go down to Egypt.
options.note: The opposite; God sanctions the descent and even blesses it.

options.text: Egypt will become the permanent home of his descendants.
options.note: God promises to bring them back, not to leave them there forever.

options.text: Joseph has died and Jacob should mourn.
options.note: God speaks of blessing and return, and Joseph is very much alive.

quiz.q: Why does the passage carefully list the seventy who went down to Egypt?

options.text: To record the covenant seed that will grow into the promised nation.
options.note: The roll of names ties this family to the great nation of the exodus.

options.text: To show that the family was too large to feed.
options.note: The list marks the covenant seed, not a feeding problem; Joseph provides for them.

options.text: Simply to fill space in the narrative.
options.note: The genealogy has real theological weight, linking the household to a nation.

options.text: To prove the brothers had abandoned Jacob.
options.note: The opposite; the whole family travels together to Joseph.

quiz.q: What is striking about Jacob's meeting with Pharaoh?

options.text: The aged, landless patriarch blesses the mighty Pharaoh, the greater blessing the lesser.
options.note: Jacob blesses Pharaoh twice, quietly signaling whose God is sovereign.

options.text: Pharaoh refuses to let the family settle.
options.note: Pharaoh generously grants them the best of the land.

options.text: Jacob demands a throne of his own.
options.note: Jacob seeks no power; he blesses Pharaoh and speaks humbly of his hard years.

options.text: Jacob hides that his sons are shepherds.
options.note: Their shepherding is openly stated and leads to their settling in Goshen.

quiz.q: How do the Egyptians respond to Joseph's provision during the famine?

options.text: They say, you have saved our lives, and become Pharaoh's servants.
options.note: Joseph's administration keeps a starving nation alive.

options.text: They revolt against Pharaoh and Joseph.
options.note: There is no revolt; they willingly sell land for food and thank Joseph.

options.text: They flee Egypt entirely.
options.note: They stay and receive seed to sow under Joseph's new arrangement.

options.text: They accuse Joseph of hoarding grain unjustly.
options.note: They credit him with saving their lives, not with injustice.

quiz.q: Why does Jacob make Joseph swear to bury him with his fathers, not in Egypt?

options.text: His hope rests in the land of promise, looking beyond death to God's covenant.
options.note: Jacob's dying request is an act of covenant faith.

options.text: He dislikes Egyptian burial customs.
options.note: The real issue is the promised land, not merely custom.

options.text: He wants to punish his sons for settling in Egypt.
options.note: It is faith in the promise, not punishment, that drives his request.

options.text: He no longer believes God's promises.
options.note: His request is precisely an expression of faith in those promises.

questions: Before the biggest move of his life, Jacob stopped to worship and heard God say, do not be afraid. What decision are you facing that you need to bring to God before taking a step?
God promised to go down to Egypt with Jacob. Where do you need to trust that God goes with you into an unfamiliar or unwanted season?
A frail old man blessed the most powerful ruler on earth. How does this reshape your sense of where real significance and authority come from?
Joseph used his position to keep others alive. How might God be calling you to use your resources or influence to provide for people in need?
Jacob died looking toward a promise he had not yet received. What promises of God are you holding onto that reach beyond what you can see now?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew counts the family as seventy; some Greek witnesses read seventy-five, the figure Stephen cites in Acts 7:14, reflecting a slightly different way of counting Joseph's descendants.
The reading that Joseph reduced the people to servitude follows the Samaritan, Greek, and Latin; the traditional Hebrew reads that he removed the people to the cities.
Israel bowed at the head of his bed follows the Hebrew; the Greek reads that he worshiped leaning on the top of his staff, the wording quoted in Hebrews 11:21.
